verses.text: If a man has a rising, scab, or bright spot like leprosy — bring him to the priest. Hair white and plague deeper than skin — leprosy. If bright but not sunken, hair not white — shut him up seven days. Priest looks again; if unchanged — seven more. If dim and not spread — scab; wash, clean. If spread — unclean. Guidance: The priest is the diagnostician — leprosy isn't dealt with by guess. Time tells the truth: seven days, then seven more. Sometimes you can't know if a thing is dangerous until you wait.

When a leprous plague is in a man, bring to the priest. White rising with white hair and raw flesh — old leprosy, unclean. If leprosy breaks out, covers head to foot, all turned white — priest pronounces clean. Raw flesh appears — unclean. When raw flesh changes to white — clean. Guidance: The strange-sounding rule: if it covers everything, it's clean. Once the disease is past hiding, it can be cleansed. Sometimes a soul has to admit it is wholly sick before God calls it well.

Where a boil was healed — if a white rising or bright reddish spot — show the priest. Sunken with white hair — leprosy from the boil. Not lower, hair not white, somewhat dark — shut up seven days. Spread — leprosy; unclean. Stayed, not spread — scab of the boil, clean. Guidance: Even an old wound can host new sickness. The priest watches a place healed long ago to see if something new has crept in. Healing once does not mean healed forever.

If by burning of fire a quick raw flesh and a bright reddish-white spot — leprosy of the burning. White hair and lower than skin — leprosy. If not, dim — shut up seven days. Spread — unclean. Stayed, dimmer — rising of the burning; clean. Guidance: Fire leaves a mark; the priest watches whether the mark is memory or living disease. Old burns don't always heal cleanly. Sometimes God sets a waiting period to find out what's underneath.

Plague on head or beard — sunken, yellow thin hair — dry scall, leprosy. Not deeper, no black hair — shut seven days. Not spread, no yellow hair — shave around (not the scall), seven more. Not spread — wash, clean. Spread after cleansing — leprosy. Black hair grown in — healed, clean. Guidance: Two weeks of waiting; a shave that respects the wound; black hair counted as a sign of life returning. Healing has small signs — God's people learn to notice them.

Bright spots in the skin of flesh, white — if dim — a freckled spot grown in the skin; clean. Guidance: Some spots are just spots. Not every mark is dangerous. God's law is careful — it sentences only what is truly diseased and absolves the rest.

Bright spots in the skin of flesh, white — if dim — a freckled spot grown in the skin; clean. Guidance: Some spots are just spots. Not every mark is dangerous. God's law is careful — it sentences only what is truly diseased and absolves the rest.

A man whose hair falls off his head is bald, yet clean. Hair fallen off the front — forehead bald, clean. But if a white reddish sore in the bald head or forehead, like leprosy — leprosy in the bald head; he is unclean. Guidance: Baldness itself is not impure. But sores that flare into a bald scalp are watched. God distinguishes between what is simply human aging and what is genuinely dangerous.

The leper — his clothes rent, head bare, upper lip covered; he shall cry: Unclean, unclean! All the days of the plague he dwells alone outside the camp. Guidance: The leper announces his own condition — Unclean! Heartbreaking, and yet it is the only way the camp stays safe. Jesus reverses this in Mark 1: instead of the leper crying, Jesus touches him, and the contagion goes the other way.

Garment plague — in wool, linen, skin, anything — show the priest. Look seven days. Spread — fretting leprosy; burn it. Not spread — wash, seven more. Color unchanged but spread — burn. Color faded after washing — rend out the plague. Reappears — burn. Cleansed — wash again, clean. Guidance: Even cloth can carry the contagion of decay. Possessions, like bodies, can be infected. God's holiness reaches into our wardrobes; some things have to be burned for the community to stay well.
